comment.body:
no, this is a misinterpretation thanks to the furus...
so vanna is a vol exposure, and so it is heavily vol and time dependent.
"long OTM call + short OTM put = net long vanna" is an oversimplification. It is true for speed profile, but not necessary true for vanna.

What i wrote is that net speed is negative (long OTM put gamma + short OTM call gamma in avg, but it also can be short both, but less short gamma downside and more short gamma upside... the key is that gamma exposure grows to the downside and decreases to the upside)
but net vanna is positive. this is bcs vanna is meassured across multiple tenors, and each of their IV changes differently.
I tested and coded that IF the volatility catches up by roughly 5% at least, the vanna profile will shift, bcs the vega and vomma profile shifts so across the strikes. Net vomma is long but locally it is different, and downside vol would react faster than upside vol across the strikes, and so it shifts the vanna profile.

the key thing is that it's all is precalculated by the participants, and hence they bet that they are interested to jump on the short skew side once vol catched up by 5% into Wednesday (like VIX to 17.5-18s and above), the higher it goes, the steeper the short vanna profile becomes. 
like sharks waiting for their prey... "vol scalpers"/"left-tail scalpers" are waiting for a panic long vol trade to short it.

i projected levels for the scenarios, based on the expected volatility (including the vol of vol selling too), in order to estimate what would a "panic long vol trade" mean in terms of spot level. 
but the focus is on the vol risk premium, not on the spot.
the positioning clearly shows: downside gamma is sold
